---
title: "6: GitHub Copilot and Claude Code setup"
subtitle: "Configuring the VS Code extensions we'll use to get genAI help"
date: 2026-07-03
date-modified: today
author:
  - name: Jelmer Poelstra
    orcid: 0000-0002-3514-7462
    email: poelstra.1@osu.edu
    affiliation: 
      - name: CFAES Bioinformatics Core, Ohio State University
        url: https://mcic.osu.edu/bioinformatics
bibliography: refs.bib # You can add reference to this files if you have and cite any
---

---------

<hr style="height:1pt; visibility:hidden;" />

## Introduction

As mentioned in the [previous section](05-setup.qmd):

> In the rest of this workshop, we'll primarily use the following set of tools to
> run code, access data, and make use of agentic AI tools:
>
> 1. A code editor, **VS Code**
> 2. A shared computing environment, **OSC** (the Ohio Supercomputer Center)
> 3. Generative and Agentic AI tools _integrated in our code editor_,
>    **GitHub Copilot** and **Claude Code**

After configuring and testing the VS Code-OSC connection in that section,
we will now set up the two agentic AI tools.
Don't despair, this should be a lot more straightforward! 

## Setting up GitHub Copilot

### Background

GitHub Copilot is an generative and agentic AI tool that can help you write code
and perform other tasks.
It's the first of two similar tools we will use in this workshop, the other being Claude Code.

GitHub Copilot is **not** the same as the "general" Microsoft Copilot.
And while GitHub Copilot _is_ a Microsoft product, hence the similar name,
you cannot –currently– use it institutionally at OSU.
Instead, access runs through a **personal GitHub account**.
It's easy and free to create a GitHub account,
and you may already have one from previous usage or because you followed 
[this optional pre-workshop setup step](s3-github-account.qmd).

Keep in mind that GitHub is a much more general platform than just GitHub Copilot,
widely used for sharing and collaborating on code.
Nevertheless, with your GitHub account,
you are nowadays also automatically enrolled in the GitHub Copilot free plan.

While there's also a stand-alone app and web-based interface for it,
**we will use GitHub Copilot within VS Code**, where it is available as a pre-installed plugin.
Therefore, little setup is needed:
the steps below only serve to log in to your GitHub account or create one along the way.

::: {.callout-tip}
#### Pros of GitHub Copilot

- For academics, there is a generous (more so for teachers than students)
  free personal option for GitHub Copilot, which is not the case for Claude Code.

- Even though it's ideal to use a tool approved for private data,
  you may not be working with private data at all, or be able to get help without exposing it.
  If so, then GitHub Copilot is a perfectly fine option.

- Seemingly minor but very handy: only GitHub Copilot has inline "code completion" features
  (_And:_ if you only use these, any data privacy concerns can be circumvented.)
:::

::: {.callout-note}
#### GitHub Copilot plans

The GitHub Copilot free plan will work for the purposes of this workshop,
but is _not_ sufficient for sustained Copilot use.

You may have done the optional pre-workshop setup step to 
[obtain GitHub Copilot Student/Pro access via your academic credentials](s3-github-account.qmd).
If that was approved (should be quick) and processed (72 hours later),
you'll now have more extensive, though not unlimited, access to GitHub Copilot.
For more details, see [our GitHub setup page](s3-github-account.qmd#apply-for-additional-free-github-copilot-access)

If you didn't do that, there isn't much point in doing it _right now_,
because you won't obtain access during the workshop given the 72-hour processing time.
But feel free to do so in a break or after the workshop.
:::

### The setup steps

1. Towards the right side of the top VS Code bar, click the **Sign In** button to log into GitHub:

   ![](/img/vscode-copilot-signin.png){fig-align="center" width="65%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code interface, showing the GitHub Copilot icon in the top right corner of the window"}

   ::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
   ### Don't see the Sign In button? _(Click to expand)_

   In the VS Code Status Bar (the thin bar way at the bottom),
   there should be a **GitHub Copilot** icon ---
   the icon to the left of the bell in the screenshot below:

   ![](/img/vscode-copilot-icon.png){fig-align="center" width="20%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code interface, showing the GitHub Copilot icon in the top right corner of the window"}

   Your version of the icon may be striked-through, which means that GitHub Copilot is not yet active.
   Click on the icon and the login pop-up should appear.
   :::

3. A pop-up similar to the left-hand image below should appear,
   and if you click on Continue with GitHub, you should see something like the right-hand image:

   ![Logging into GitHub to use GitHub Copilot. [From the GitHub Blog](https://github.blog/changelog/2025-07-15-social-login-with-google-is-now-generally-available).](img/github-login-vscode.png){fig-align="center" width="80%"}

   ::: callout-tip
   #### GitHub account options

   - For those **without** a GitHub account, you can create one on the fly, either with
     the "Create an account" button on the bottom, or semi-automatically via an existing
     Google login by clicking the "Continue with Google" button.

   - For those **with** a GitHub account, you can log in with your existing credentials.
     Don't use Continue with Google unless you know your GitHub account is already linked to your Google account,
     or account confusion may arise.
   :::

   ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
   ### Were you already signed in to GitHub? Then the prompt should look like this _(Click to expand)_
   
   ![](/img/copilot-already-signed-in.png){fig-align="center" width="55%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code interface, showing the GitHub Copilot login prompt"}
   :::

4. Finally, you may be prompted to authorize VS Code to access your GitHub account, which you should do:

![](/img/copilot-signin-send-back.png){fig-align="center" width="45%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the GitHub login page, showing the 'Authorize Visual Studio Code' button highlighted"}

5. GitHub Copilot lives in a "Secondary Side Bar" on the righthand side,
   which you can toggle by pressing
   the rightmost icon in the screenshot below, on the right side of the top VS Code bar:

   ![Clicking the rightmost icon toggles the Secondary Side Bar with GitHub Copilot. _TBA: highlight icon_](/img/vscode-copilot-sidebar.png){fig-align="center" width="35%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code interface, showing the GitHub Copilot sidebar"}

   That AI chat sidebar should look something like this:

   ![](/img/vscode-copilot-blank.png){fig-align="center" width="50%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code interface, showing the GitHub Copilot sidebar"}

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
As mentioned above, unless you have already [obtained GitHub Copilot Student/Pro access via your academic credentials](s3-github-account.qmd),
you will be automatically using the GitHub Copilot free plan after logging in or creating a new account.
:::

### One example prompt

Let's give it one quick try, before we go into more detail in the next sessions.
In the Copilot prompt box
(which should have the ghost text "_Describe what to build_"), enter:

> _How many reads are in data/iqbal/SRR31869590_1.fastq?_

Because we're asking Copilot for information it can only get after actually doing
something (running a command), it will by default ask for permission to do so:

![](/img/copilot-asks-permission.png){fig-align="center" width="70%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the GitHub Copilot prompt asking for permission to run a command"}

Click `Allow` and it will run the command and return the answer:

![](/img/copilot-readnr-answer.png){fig-align="center" width="70%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the GitHub Copilot prompt showing the answer to the question about how many reads are in the specified fastq file"}

#### What did we just witness?
This example shows how in-editor AI like GitHub Copilot can perform actions
themselves, rather than just providing information or code snippets.
In technical terms, as part of this agentic behavior, it "calls a tool"
(or: "performs **tool calls**") that allow it to run arbitrary commands in the terminal.

We'll soon see another type of extremely useful in-editor agentic behavior,
where it can **read and/or edit your code**.
This latter type of behavior is what we'll use most in this workshop.
That's because broadly speaking, the example above notwithstanding,
_the goal in this workshop is not to let AI analyze the data for us,_
_but rather to let it help us write code to appropriately analyze the data with_
_existing non-AI programs._

## Setting up Claude Code

### Background

**Like with GitHub Copilot**, the Anthropic product Claude Code comes in various forms,
but we will use it within VS Code, where it is available as a plugin.

**Unlike with GitHub Copilot**, we will not use a personal account to access Claude Code.
Instead, we will use an access key that the workshop instructors have obtained
using OSU OTDI's [Generative AI Service](https://it.osu.edu/generative-ai-service).
Moving forward, we will refer to this type of access key as the "**OSU LiteLLM API key**"^[
It runs via the LiteLLM service, and these kind of keys in general are called "API keys".].

The advantages of using Claude Code via the OSU LiteLLM API key
---not just during this workshop, but for you moving forward--- are:

1. It is approved at OSU for use with any type of institutional data (S2–S4).
   <span style="font-size: 1.2em;">🎉</span>
3. OTDI currently has an initiative where **anyone at OSU can get $100 worth of AI credits**
   via their own OSU LiteLLM API key (see the bottom of this page). <span style="font-size: 1.2em;">🥳</span>
2. At least in principle, you can get a work-related rather than personal funding
   source for additional (as in, beyond this workshop and beyond your free key)
   AI usage.

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
#### Don't get confused about data approval contexts _(Click to expand)_
Claude Code is **only** approved for use with institutional data when it is accessed
via an access key (API key) from OTDI's Generative AI Service.
If you pay for a personal Anthropic account and use that to access Claude Code,
you are **not** using it in an approved way. 
:::

### The setup steps

1. In the **Extensions** view, search for "claude" and install the extension
   "[Claude Code for VS Code](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=anthropic.claude-code)":

![](/img/vscode-claude-install.png){fig-align="center" width="50%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code Extensions sidebar, showing the Claude Code extension highlighted"}

2. To authenticate to Claude Code, we will use a settings file where we tell Claude
   about our API key and settings associated with it.
   **In the terminal**, copy this file to your Home directory
   (where it will be automatically read by Claude Code):

   ```bash
   cp /fs/scratch/PAS3454/share/settings.json ~/.claude/settings.json
   ```

   ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
   #### What does this file contain? _(Click to expand)_

   We can't share this file on the website in full,
   because it contains an API key that cannot be exposed publicly.
   But for your reference, the contents of this file
   minus the API key (here replaced by `REPLACE_WITH_API_KEY`) are as follows:
   
   ```json
   {
   "theme": "auto",
   "env": {                                                                                                                                                                                        
       "ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL": "https://litellm.cloud.osu.edu",                                                                                                                                        
       "ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN": <REPLACE_WITH_API_KEY>,
       "ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_OPUS_MODEL": "claude-opus-4-6",
       "ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_SONNET_MODEL": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
       "ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_HAIKU_MODEL": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
       "CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_EXPERIMENTAL_BETAS": "1"                                                                                                                                        
       }   
   }
   ```
   :::

3. A second tab should now be available in the side bar with Copilot,
   named "CLAUDE" --- click on that
   (_if you don't see it, you may have to widen the side bar!_):

   ![Next to "CHAT" (i.e., GitHub Copilot), a "CLAUDE CODE" tab is now available.](/img/vscode-claude-in-sidebar.png){fig-align="center" width="70%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code interface, showing the Claude Code icon in the top right corner of the window"}

   ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
   #### Alternative way of opening a Claude Code window _(Click to expand)_
   
   Once installed, a Claude Code icon will appear in the topright of the editor pane:
   
   ![](/img/vscode-claude-icon.png){fig-align="center" width="20%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the VS Code interface, showing the Claude Code icon in the top right corner of the window"}
   
   Clicking this icon will also open a Claude Code window,
   but this one will be opened within the editor pane rather than in the sidebar.
   :::

4. You should for some moments see the following messages in the Claude Code window,
   indicating that you are not authenticated yet:
   
   ![](/img/claude-login.png){fig-align="center" width="50%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the Claude Code interface, showing the first window after logging in, with a message indicating that you are not authenticated yet"}

5. Simply wait, and if all goes well, Claude will process the settings file behind
   the scenes, and switch to an interface similar to the Copilot one:

   ![](/img/claude-first-window.png){fig-align="center" width="55%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the Claude Code interface, showing the first window after logging in"}

   ::: {.callout-important}
   #### Does the window with login options not automatically disappear?
   Don't try to log in via the options in that window,
   but warn the instructors and we'll troubleshoot.
   :::

All done!

::: exercise
#### {{< fa user-edit >}} Exercise: Test that Claude Code works

In the prompt box at the bottom,
go ahead and ask it a quick, simple question to test that it works.

No inspiration? Ask a classic stumbling-block question for AIs, such as
_"How many b's are in the word blueberry?"_
:::

::: callout-tip
### Getting and using your own, free API key!

OSU currently has a initiative where anyone at OSU can get $100 worth of AI credits
via their own OSU LiteLLM API key.

This API key is **_valid until the end of September_** and can be used in the same
way as we do in the workshop,
as well as in other contexts that we have not shown in this workshop.

1. Go to <https://go.osu.edu/api-fluency> and log in
2. Click "**Redeem my free key**"



![](/img/free-api-key.png){fig-align="center" width="70%" fig-alt="Screenshot of the OSU Generative AI Service website, showing the 'Redeem my free key' button highlighted"}
:::
